_**Agent (default)**_

I can’t see your private Vercel build logs from the [project overview URL](https://vercel.com/sjp10-9620s-projects/harmonyforge-web) alone. What *is* visible from that kind of page (and from the snippet you shared) is a **Production checklist** that still says things like **“Connect Git Repository”**, which usually means either Git isn’t linked yet or the project was created without a proper Git connection—so the “issue” may be **no working deploy pipeline**, not a failed build.

Here is a tight way to narrow it down, aligned with your own **[`docs/deployment.md`](https://github.com/shivpat/harmonyforge/blob/main/docs/deployment.md)** (HarmonyForge’s Vercel app is the **Next.js app under `frontend/`**).

## 1. Confirm what is actually wrong

In Vercel, open **Deployments** → click the latest deployment → copy the **Build log** (or a screenshot of the red error). Without that line, everything below is educated guessing.

## 2. Settings that break this repo most often

| Setting | What HarmonyForge expects | If wrong |
|--------|---------------------------|----------|
| **Root Directory** | **`frontend`** | Vercel builds from repo root → no `next` app / wrong `package.json` → install or build fails. |
| **Framework** | Next.js (auto) | Usually fine if root is `frontend`. |
| **Git** | Repo connected to the right GitHub project | No auto deploys; checklist stays on “Connect Git”. |

Your internal doc is explicit: *“Create a new Vercel project (**root directory = frontend**).”*

## 3. Evidence from this workspace

- A local **`npm run build`** in `frontend/` **succeeds** (Next 16.1.6), so a typical Vercel failure here is more likely **project configuration** (root dir, Node, install) or **environment/build-time** assumptions—not an obvious broken app compile.
- You have **`postinstall`: `patch-package`** and a committed patch under `frontend/patches/`; that’s fine **as long as `frontend/patches` is in Git** (if it weren’t, Vercel would fail on install).

## 4. Node version

If the log mentions an unsupported Node version, set **Project → Settings → General → Node.js Version** to a current **20.x or 22.x** LTS (Next 16 expects a modern Node).

## 5. Runtime vs “deployment” confusion

After it deploys, **PDF/OMR** paths may still misbehave on Vercel (no `pdfalto`/Poppler); that’s a **runtime/feature** limitation described in `docs/deployment.md`, not necessarily a build failure.

---

**Next step:** Paste the **exact error block** from the failed deployment’s build log (or say whether the problem is “no deployments”, “build failed”, or “deploy OK but site errors”). With that, we can map it to a concrete fix (root directory, env vars, install, etc.).

Superman — once you have that log snippet, drop it here and we can tie it to the precise setting or code path.

_**User**_

This is the only error I amn getting
404: NOT_FOUND
Code: NOT_FOUND
ID: cle1::qxqz7-1776541864184-c1c78eb0637a

Read our documentation to learn more about this error.

---

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user encounters a 404 NOT_FOUND error on Vercel, accompanied by an error ID. This is Vercel's standard 404 error page, not a Next.js application-level 404. Common causes include:

- Deployment doesn't exist (deleted or failed deployment)
- Wrong URL (invalid project or expired branch preview)
- No production deployment
- Domain misconfiguration (custom domain pointing to invalid location)</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

That message is **Vercel’s platform response**, not your Next.js app’s normal “page not found.” It means **nothing is being served for that hostname/URL** at the edge—most often because **there is no successful production deployment**, the URL is wrong, or the deployment was removed.

### What to check (in order)

1. **Deployments**  
   Open the project → **Deployments**.  
   - If the list is empty or everything is **Error/Canceled**, fix the latest failed build first—until you have at least one **Ready** deployment, the default URL will often show **NOT_FOUND**.

2. **Exact URL**  
   Use the URL Vercel shows on a **successful** deployment (usually  
   `https://<project-name>.vercel.app`  
   or the deployment’s own preview URL).  
   Typos, old bookmarked preview URLs, or a wrong team/project name all produce this.

3. **Git / production branch**  
   If **Production** has no deployment from your main branch (or whatever branch you set as Production), connect the repo and deploy, or run **`vercel --prod`** from `frontend/` so Production isn’t empty.

4. **Custom domain**  
   If you use a custom domain, confirm in **Settings → Domains** that it’s **Valid** and assigned to a deployment that **exists and is Ready**. A domain pointing at the project with no live deployment can still yield **NOT_FOUND**.

### How to tell app 404 vs this

- **This Vercel error:** Plain “404: NOT_FOUND” with **Code: NOT_FOUND** and an **ID** like `cle1::…` — usually **no (or wrong) deployment**.  
- **Your app:** You’d see your **HarmonyForge layout** / `not-found` UI, not Vercel’s minimal error page.

**Next step:** In the dashboard, open **Deployments**, click the latest **Ready** deployment, open its URL from that screen, and say whether that works. If there is **no Ready** deployment, paste the **build** error from the latest failed one—that’s the real blocker before this 404 goes away.

Superman — once you know whether any deployment is **Ready**, we can narrow it to “empty production” vs “wrong URL/domain” in one step.
